lessons.title: When a Cable Is More Than a Wire
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Think about the cable that runs from your wall to your TV, or from your Wi-Fi router to its antenna. You might picture it as 'just a wire' that carries a signal from one end to the other. For slow, everyday electricity that picture is fine. But for fast, high-frequency signals (the kind radios, TVs, and routers use) something more interesting happens.

**Build.** A *signal* is a pattern of electrical energy. At high frequencies, the signal does not just appear at the far end of the cable instantly. It actually *travels along the cable as a wave* (a moving ripple of energy), the same way a ripple travels down a rope when you flick one end. Because of this, we stop calling the cable 'a wire' and call it a **transmission line**: a cable built to carry a signal cleanly as a traveling wave.

Every transmission line has a built-in number called its **characteristic impedance**. *Impedance* (measured in ohms, symbol Ω) describes how strongly the line pushes back on the signal as voltage (electrical 'pressure') and current (electrical 'flow') ride along it together. The characteristic impedance is set by the cable's *shape and materials* (how thick the inner wire is, how far the outer shield sits, what insulator fills the gap), not by its length. Two everyday values you will see printed on cables are **50 ohms** (common for radio and Wi-Fi gear) and **75 ohms** (common for TV and video coax). A '50-ohm cable' simply means a cable whose characteristic impedance is 50 ohms.

**Recap.** At high frequency a cable is not just a wire: the signal travels along it as a wave, and the cable's shape gives it a characteristic impedance (a number in ohms, like 50 or 75) describing how the signal rides along it.

items.prompt: At high frequencies, how does a signal move along a transmission line?
choices: It travels along the cable as a wave, taking real time to reach the other end
It appears at the far end instantly, with no travel time
It stays still and only the cable moves
It only exists at the two ends, never in between
items.explanation: The key shift in thinking: at high frequency the signal is a traveling wave moving down the cable, like a ripple down a rope. That is exactly why we treat the cable as a 'transmission line' rather than a plain wire. It does not arrive instantly.

items.prompt: What is the 'characteristic impedance' of a cable?
choices: A number (in ohms) describing how voltage and current ride along the line together
The total length of the cable in meters
The color of the cable's outer jacket
The amount of power the source produces
items.explanation: Characteristic impedance, measured in ohms, describes how the line carries a signal as paired voltage and current. It is a property of the cable itself, not a measure of length, color, or source power.

items.prompt: Two common characteristic-impedance values printed on real-world cables are:
choices: 50 ohms and 75 ohms
1 ohm and 2 ohms
100 volts and 200 volts
5 meters and 10 meters
items.explanation: 50-ohm cable is common for radio and Wi-Fi equipment; 75-ohm cable is common for TV and video. These are impedances in ohms, not volts or meters.

items.prompt: What mainly sets a cable's characteristic impedance?
choices: Its shape and materials (wire thickness, shield spacing, insulator)
How long the cable is
How loud the signal is
The time of day it is used
items.explanation: Characteristic impedance comes from the cable's physical build: conductor sizes, spacing, and the insulator between them. A 50-ohm cable is 50 ohms whether it is short or long, because length does not change the impedance.

items.prompt: Why is a high-frequency cable called a 'transmission line' instead of just 'a wire'?
choices: Because the signal travels along it as a wave, so the cable's wave behavior matters
Because it is always longer than a normal wire
Because it cannot carry electricity at all
Because it has no metal inside
items.explanation: The name reflects behavior: the signal rides the cable as a traveling wave, so we must think about how that wave moves and what the cable does to it, not just treat the cable as an instant connection.

items.prompt: A friend says, 'My cable is 50 ohms, so a longer one must be 100 ohms.' What is wrong with this?
choices: Characteristic impedance does not depend on length; a longer 50-ohm cable is still 50 ohms
Longer cables are actually 25 ohms
Impedance is measured in volts, not ohms
Cables have no impedance at all
items.explanation: Characteristic impedance is set by the cable's cross-section and materials, which do not change with length. Cutting or extending a 50-ohm cable keeps it 50 ohms.

items.prompt: In simple terms, what does 'impedance' (in ohms) describe for a transmission line?
choices: How the line pushes back on the signal as voltage and current ride along it
How heavy the cable is
How brightly the cable glows
How far apart its two ends are
items.explanation: Impedance, in ohms, captures the relationship between the electrical 'pressure' (voltage) and 'flow' (current) of the signal on the line. It is not about weight, glow, or distance.

items.prompt: The coax cable behind a TV is best described as:
choices: A transmission line, typically 75 ohms, that carries the signal as a wave
A solid bar of metal with no insulation
A 12-volt power supply
A wire whose impedance changes with the weather
items.explanation: TV coax is a real transmission line, usually 75 ohms, built to carry high-frequency signals cleanly as traveling waves. Its characteristic impedance is fixed by its construction.

lessons.title: The Golden Rule: Matching Impedance
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You have a source (something producing a signal, like a transmitter), a cable, and a load (something using the signal, like an antenna or speaker). You want *all* the signal's power to reach the load and none to be wasted. There is one simple rule that makes that happen.

**Build.** The **golden rule of transmission lines**: to deliver all the power, the source, the cable, and the load should all have the *same* impedance. When they match, the signal flows smoothly from source to load and every bit of power is delivered. We call this an **impedance match** (or just 'matched').

Here is the picture. A 50-ohm source feeds a 50-ohm cable, which feeds a 50-ohm load. Everything agrees on how voltage and current should ride along, so the wave glides straight into the load and is absorbed there. That is the goal: maximum power delivered, nothing bounced back.

Now suppose the load is *wrong* (say a 150-ohm load on a 50-ohm line). The signal arrives expecting 50 ohms but meets 150 ohms, so it cannot all fit into the load. Part of the signal is turned around and sent *back* down the cable. This is a **mismatch**, and the part that comes back is a **reflection** (we explore reflections next lesson). Reflected power is wasted power: it never reaches the load.

This is why equipment is built around standard impedances. A 50-ohm radio, 50-ohm cable, and 50-ohm antenna are designed to match so the radio's power actually goes out the antenna.

**Recap.** The golden rule: matching impedance (source = cable = load) delivers all the power. A mismatch sends part of the signal back as a wasteful reflection.

items.prompt: What is the 'golden rule' for delivering all the power to a load?
choices: The source, cable, and load should all have matching impedance
The cable should be as long as possible
The load impedance should be much larger than the cable's
The source should be turned off briefly first
items.explanation: When source, cable, and load impedances all match, the signal flows smoothly and all the power is delivered. Mismatches, not length or large loads, are what cause wasted power.

items.prompt: On a 50-ohm line, what load gives a perfect match (all power delivered, nothing reflected)?
choices: A 50-ohm load
A 150-ohm load
A 5-ohm load
An open end (no load at all)
items.explanation: A load equal to the line's impedance (50 ohms here) is the matched case: the wave is fully absorbed and no power bounces back. The other choices are all mismatches.

items.prompt: What happens to the signal when a 150-ohm load sits on a 50-ohm line?
choices: Part of the signal reflects back down the cable, wasting power
All the power is delivered, exactly as in a match
The cable's impedance changes to 150 ohms
The signal speeds up to make up the difference
items.explanation: A mismatch means the load cannot accept the whole wave on the line's terms, so part of the signal is sent back as a reflection. That reflected power never reaches the load and is wasted.

items.prompt: Why is reflected power a problem?
choices: It is power that never reaches the load, so it is wasted
It makes the load run cooler and is helpful
It permanently destroys the cable
It increases the total power delivered
items.explanation: Reflected power travels back toward the source instead of into the load, reducing the power actually delivered. It does not boost delivery or help the load.

items.prompt: Why are radios, cables, and antennas often all built to 50 ohms?
choices: So they match each other and the radio's power actually reaches the antenna
Because 50 ohms is the only impedance that exists
To make the cables heavier
So the signal travels faster than light
items.explanation: Using a common standard impedance lets the parts match, satisfying the golden rule so power flows from source to antenna. It is a design choice, not the only possible value.

items.prompt: In a fully matched system, how much of the signal's power reaches the load (ideally)?
choices: All of it, with nothing reflected back
Half of it, the other half always reflects
None of it
It depends on the cable's color
items.explanation: A perfect match means the wave is fully absorbed by the load: maximum power delivered, no reflection. Half-loss or total loss describes mismatched, not matched, conditions.

items.prompt: Which describes an 'impedance mismatch'?
choices: The load impedance differs from the line impedance, so part of the signal reflects
The source and load impedances are exactly equal
The cable is exactly one meter long
The signal has no voltage
items.explanation: A mismatch is any case where impedances do not agree (for example a 150-ohm load on a 50-ohm line), which causes a reflection. Equal impedances are a match, not a mismatch.

items.prompt: A 50-ohm source, 50-ohm cable, and 50-ohm antenna are connected. What should you expect?
choices: A good match: the wave flows into the antenna with no reflection
A bad mismatch with most power reflected
The antenna will refuse any power
The cable impedance will jump to 150 ohms
items.explanation: All three parts agree at 50 ohms, satisfying the golden rule, so the signal is delivered cleanly with essentially no reflected power.

lessons.title: Reflections from a Mismatched End
lessons.content:
**Hook.** You already know that a wave partly bounces back when it meets a boundary (the same idea you met with waves at a barrier). A transmission line is no different: a mismatched load acts like a boundary, and the signal reflects off it. A simple rope makes this easy to feel.

**Build.** Imagine flicking a wave down a rope.

- **Rope tied tightly to a wall.** The wave hits the wall, can't go anywhere, and bounces straight back at you. The wall is a *total* mismatch.
- **Rope blended smoothly into a perfectly matching rope.** The wave glides across the join and keeps going. There is nothing to bounce off; this is a *match*, and the wave passes through.

A transmission line behaves the same way. The load is the 'far end' of the rope:

- **Matched load** (load = line impedance): like the smooth join. No reflection; all power passes into the load.
- **Open end** (the cable just stops, nothing connected): like a free, untied rope end. The wave reflects fully back.
- **Short** (the two conductors touched together): also a full reflection, like tying the rope hard to the wall.
- **Wrong-impedance load** (e.g. 150 ohms on a 50-ohm line): a *partial* reflection. Some power passes into the load, some bounces back.

The rule of thumb, stated plainly: **the reflection is zero when the load equals the line impedance, and the further the load is from that value, the bigger the reflection.** Open and short ends are the extreme mismatches, so they reflect everything; a load close to the line impedance reflects only a little.

**Recap.** A mismatched end reflects the signal, just like a rope hitting a wall. Matched = no reflection; open or short = full reflection; a wrong-but-finite load = partial reflection. Bigger mismatch means bigger reflection.

items.prompt: Flicking a wave down a rope tied tightly to a wall, what happens?
choices: The wave reflects fully back toward you
The wave passes through the wall unchanged
The wave disappears with no reflection
The wave speeds up after the wall
items.explanation: The wall is a hard boundary the wave cannot cross, so the wave bounces back. This is the everyday picture of a total reflection from a complete mismatch.

items.prompt: A rope blended smoothly into a perfectly matching rope is the analogy for what line condition?
choices: A matched load: the wave passes through with no reflection
A short circuit with full reflection
An open end with full reflection
A cable with no signal
items.explanation: A smooth join gives the wave nothing to bounce off, so it passes through. That mirrors a matched load (load = line impedance), where no power is reflected.

items.prompt: When does a transmission line have zero reflection?
choices: When the load impedance equals the line's characteristic impedance
When the load is an open end
When the load is a short
When the cable is very long
items.explanation: Zero reflection happens only when the load matches the line impedance. Open and short ends are extreme mismatches that reflect everything, and length does not set the reflection.

items.prompt: An open end (cable just stops, nothing connected) causes:
choices: A full reflection of the signal back down the line
No reflection, like a matched load
A small partial reflection only
The signal to leave the cable into the air with no loss
items.explanation: An open end is an extreme mismatch (like a free rope end), so the wave reflects fully back. It is the opposite of a matched load.

items.prompt: A 150-ohm load on a 50-ohm line (a wrong-but-finite load) produces:
choices: A partial reflection: some power passes into the load, some bounces back
No reflection at all
A full reflection, like an open or short
More delivered power than a matched load
items.explanation: Because the load is mismatched but not extreme, part of the wave enters the load and part reflects. Only a matched load gives zero reflection; only open/short give full reflection.

items.prompt: How does the size of the reflection depend on the load?
choices: The further the load is from the line impedance, the bigger the reflection
The reflection is the same for every load
Reflection shrinks as the mismatch grows
Reflection depends only on cable color
items.explanation: Reflection is zero at a perfect match and grows as the load moves away from the line impedance. The biggest reflections come from the most extreme mismatches (open and short).

items.prompt: Why is a mismatched load like a boundary that a wave meets?
choices: Because the wave partly reflects there, just as it does at a boundary
Because it changes the cable's length
Because it makes the wave travel faster than light
Because it has no effect on the wave
items.explanation: You already know waves partly reflect at a boundary. A mismatched load is exactly that kind of boundary for the signal on a line, so part of the wave bounces back.

items.prompt: Which pair of line endings both cause a FULL reflection?
choices: An open end and a short
A matched load and an open end
A matched load and a short
Two matched loads
items.explanation: Both an open end and a short are extreme mismatches, so each reflects the whole wave. A matched load, by contrast, reflects nothing.

lessons.title: Standing Waves & the VSWR Number
lessons.content:
**Hook.** When a line is mismatched, you get two waves on it at once: the original wave heading toward the load, and the reflected wave heading back. What happens when two waves share the same cable? They add together, and the result is a surprisingly tidy, fixed pattern.

**Build.** The wave going toward the load is the **incident wave**. The wave bouncing back is the **reflected wave**. At every point on the line these two waves add up. In some spots they reinforce each other (giving a strong signal); in other spots they cancel (giving a weak signal). Because both waves keep moving at a steady rate, these strong and weak spots sit at *fixed positions* along the cable. This locked-in pattern of high and low signal is called a **standing wave** (it appears to 'stand still' even though it is built from two moving waves).

If you could measure the signal strength all along the line, you'd see it rise and fall: high points (maxima) and low points (minima) repeating. The bigger the reflection, the deeper the dips and the taller the peaks.

We summarize 'how bad is the mismatch' with one number, the **VSWR** (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio). Plainly, it is the ratio of the strongest spot to the weakest spot on the line:

$$\text{VSWR} = \frac{\text{biggest signal}}{\text{smallest signal}}$$

VSWR is always a number **1 or greater**. A VSWR of **1 means a perfect match** (no reflection, so the signal is the same everywhere, with no peaks or dips). A **higher VSWR means a worse mismatch** (2 is some mismatch, 5 is bad, very large means almost everything is reflected). When a technician checks an antenna, VSWR is the first number they read: close to 1 is the goal.

**Recap.** Incident and reflected waves add to make a standing wave (fixed high and low spots). VSWR is one simple number for how bad the mismatch is: 1 is perfect, higher is worse.

items.prompt: What two waves combine to make a standing wave on a mismatched line?
choices: The incident wave (toward the load) and the reflected wave (coming back)
Two waves both heading toward the load
Sound waves and light waves
A wave and its echo from a different cable
items.explanation: A standing wave forms when the incident wave and the reflected wave share the line and add together. It needs both directions of travel; without a reflection there is no standing wave.

items.prompt: Why is the pattern called a 'standing' wave?
choices: The high and low spots sit at fixed positions and appear to stand still
The cable physically stands upright
The wave stops moving energy entirely
It only happens when the equipment is standing on a table
items.explanation: Although it is built from two moving waves, the resulting peaks and dips stay at fixed places along the line, so the pattern looks stationary, hence 'standing wave'.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is VSWR?
choices: The ratio of the strongest signal spot to the weakest signal spot on the line
The total length of the cable
The speed of the wave
The color code of the connector
items.explanation: VSWR (Voltage Standing Wave Ratio) compares the biggest signal on the line to the smallest. A large ratio means deep peaks and dips, signaling a strong reflection.

items.prompt: What does a VSWR of exactly 1 mean?
choices: A perfect match: no reflection, signal the same everywhere
The worst possible mismatch
Half the power is reflected
The cable is broken
items.explanation: VSWR = 1 means the biggest and smallest spots are equal, so there is no standing-wave ripple at all. That only happens with a perfect match and zero reflection.

items.prompt: A line shows a VSWR of 5. Compared with a VSWR of 1.2, this means:
choices: A worse mismatch, with more of the signal reflected
A better match
Exactly the same match
No signal on the line
items.explanation: Higher VSWR means a worse mismatch and more reflected power. A VSWR of 1.2 is close to perfect; 5 indicates a serious mismatch.

items.prompt: Can VSWR ever be less than 1?
choices: No. VSWR is always 1 or greater
Yes, a good match gives 0.5
Yes, it can be negative
Yes, it ranges from -1 to +1
items.explanation: VSWR is the ratio of the largest spot to the smallest, so it cannot drop below 1 (the best case, a perfect match). Values below 1 or negative values are not possible.

items.prompt: How does a bigger reflection change the standing-wave pattern?
choices: The peaks get taller and the dips get deeper (the ripple is stronger)
The pattern flattens out completely
The peaks and dips swap places randomly
Nothing changes
items.explanation: A larger reflection makes the incident and reflected waves reinforce and cancel more strongly, deepening the dips and raising the peaks, which shows up as a higher VSWR.

items.prompt: When a technician tests an antenna, why is VSWR the first number they check?
choices: It quickly tells how well the antenna is matched (close to 1 is good)
It tells how long the cable is
It measures the room temperature
It shows the antenna's color
items.explanation: VSWR is a fast, single-number health check of the match. A value near 1 means little reflected power and good delivery; a high value warns of a mismatch to fix.

lessons.title: Where the Power Goes: Return Loss
lessons.content:
**Hook.** When a line is mismatched and part of the signal reflects, where does that power actually go, and how do engineers describe how much comes back? There is a second common number, alongside VSWR, that answers this.

**Build.** Think of the power leaving the source as splitting into two parts at a mismatched load:

- **Delivered power** goes into the load and does its job (drives the antenna, the speaker, and so on).
- **Reflected power** bounces back toward the source. It is the wasted part, and on a badly matched line it can even cause an *echo*: the signal returns to the source like a shout coming back off a canyon wall.

The better the match, the more goes into 'delivered' and the less into 'reflected'. A perfect match (VSWR = 1) sends everything into the load and reflects nothing.

Engineers often describe the reflected part with a number called **return loss**. In plain words, return loss tells you *how much smaller the reflected signal is than the original*. A **large return loss is good**: it means only a tiny fraction comes back (a good match). A **small return loss is bad**: a big fraction returns (a poor match). So the two everyday numbers point in opposite directions: for a good match you want **VSWR close to 1** and **return loss large**. They are just two ways of saying the same thing, 'how much is reflected'.

A quick sanity check: a perfect match means no reflected signal, the largest possible return loss, and VSWR = 1. A total reflection (open or short end) means everything comes back, the smallest return loss, and a huge VSWR.

**Recap.** At a mismatch, power splits into delivered (useful) and reflected (wasted, possibly an echo). Return loss measures the reflected part: large return loss = good match; it always agrees with a VSWR near 1.

items.prompt: At a mismatched load, the source's power splits into which two parts?
choices: Delivered power (into the load) and reflected power (back to the source)
Red power and blue power
Power that vanishes and power that doubles
Only delivered power, nothing else
items.explanation: A mismatch lets some power into the load (delivered) and sends the rest back (reflected). The reflected portion is the wasted part.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what does 'return loss' describe?
choices: How much smaller the reflected signal is than the original
How long the cable is
How fast the wave travels
How heavy the connector is
items.explanation: Return loss is a measure of the reflected signal's size compared with the original. A bigger return loss means a smaller reflection, which is a better match.

items.prompt: Is a LARGE return loss good or bad?
choices: Good: only a tiny fraction of the signal reflects
Bad: almost all the signal reflects
Neither: return loss does not relate to matching
Bad: it means the cable is too long
items.explanation: Large return loss means the reflected signal is much smaller than the original, so little power comes back. That is exactly what a good match looks like.

items.prompt: For a GOOD match, what should the two everyday numbers look like?
choices: VSWR close to 1 and return loss large
VSWR very large and return loss small
VSWR close to 1 and return loss small
Both numbers equal to zero
items.explanation: A good match reflects little power, which shows as VSWR near 1 and a large return loss. The two numbers always agree because they both describe how much is reflected.

items.prompt: What can reflected power cause on a badly matched line?
choices: An echo, where the signal returns toward the source
The cable to get longer
The signal to leave the cable as light
More power delivered to the load
items.explanation: Reflected power travels back toward the source, which can act like an echo (a returned signal). It reduces, rather than increases, the power delivered to the load.

items.prompt: A perfect match (VSWR = 1) corresponds to which return loss situation?
choices: The largest possible return loss (essentially no signal reflected)
The smallest possible return loss
A negative cable length
Half the power reflected
items.explanation: With a perfect match, nothing reflects, so the reflected signal is as small as it can be, which is the largest return loss. VSWR = 1 and large return loss go together.

items.prompt: A total reflection (an open or short end) means:
choices: Everything reflects: smallest return loss and a huge VSWR
Nothing reflects: largest return loss and VSWR = 1
Half reflects and half is delivered
The power is delivered perfectly
items.explanation: An open or short end sends the whole wave back, so the reflected signal is as large as possible (smallest return loss) and the VSWR is very large. No power reaches the load.

items.prompt: Why do VSWR and return loss 'point in opposite directions' for a good match?
choices: A good match makes VSWR small (near 1) but return loss large; both still mean little reflection
Because they measure completely unrelated things
Because one is in meters and the other in seconds
Because a good match makes both numbers large
items.explanation: They describe the same reflection from two angles: less reflection lowers VSWR toward 1 and raises return loss. Different directions, same meaning.

lessons.title: Kinds of Lines, Speed & What's Next
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Transmission lines come in several shapes, and the signal on them has one more surprising property: it travels a bit *slower* than light. This lesson rounds out the picture and points to where the story continues.

**Build.** Three common line types you'll meet:

- **Coax** (coaxial cable): a center wire surrounded by an insulator and an outer shield (the round cable behind a TV or on a radio). The shield keeps the signal in and interference out.
- **Twisted pair**: two wires twisted together (think of the cable in a wired internet/Ethernet connection or an old phone line). The twisting helps cancel out noise.
- **Microstrip**: a flat copper strip running over a ground layer on a circuit board. This is how signals are routed *inside* electronics, printed right onto the board.

Each is still a transmission line with its own characteristic impedance, set by its shape and materials.

Next, **speed**. A signal in empty space travels at the speed of light, but inside a cable it goes *slower*, because the insulator around the conductors slows the wave. We capture this with the **velocity factor**: a fraction telling you what portion of light-speed the signal travels at. A velocity factor of 0.66, for example, means the signal moves at about 66% of the speed of light. This also causes a small **propagation delay**: the signal takes a tiny but real time to get from one end to the other.

Finally, **what's next**. When a load doesn't match the line, we don't always replace it; instead we add a small circuit called a **matching network** that nudges the impedance back to a match (the details come later). The classic tool for designing those matches is the **Smith chart**, a clever circular graph that makes matching visual. That is the very next sub-topic.

**Recap.** Common lines are coax, twisted pair, and microstrip, each with its own impedance. Signals travel slower than light (the velocity factor) with a small delay. Matching networks fix mismatches, and the Smith chart, coming next, is the tool for designing them.

items.prompt: Which describes a coaxial (coax) cable?
choices: A center wire surrounded by an insulator and an outer shield
Two bare wires twisted together
A flat strip printed on a circuit board
A hollow metal pipe with nothing inside
items.explanation: Coax has a center conductor, an insulating dielectric, and an outer shield. The shield contains the signal and blocks interference. Twisted pair and microstrip are different line types.

items.prompt: A wired Ethernet cable, with wires twisted together, is an example of:
choices: Twisted pair
Coax
Microstrip
A waveguide pipe
items.explanation: Twisting two wires together forms a twisted pair; the twist helps cancel noise. Coax uses a shield, and microstrip is a flat trace on a board.

items.prompt: What is 'microstrip'?
choices: A flat copper strip over a ground layer, printed on a circuit board
A round cable with a braided shield
A pair of twisted wires
A glass optical fiber
items.explanation: Microstrip is a printed flat conductor over a ground plane on a circuit board, used to route high-frequency signals inside electronics. It is still a transmission line with its own impedance.

items.prompt: How fast does a signal travel inside a typical cable, compared with the speed of light?
choices: A bit slower than light
Exactly the speed of light
Faster than light
It does not move at all
items.explanation: The insulator around the conductors slows the wave, so the signal travels somewhat slower than light in empty space. Nothing carries the signal faster than light.

items.prompt: What does a 'velocity factor' of 0.66 mean?
choices: The signal travels at about 66% of the speed of light in that cable
The cable is 66 meters long
66% of the power is reflected
The cable works only 66% of the time
items.explanation: Velocity factor is the fraction of light-speed at which the signal travels in the cable. 0.66 means about 66% of c. It is not a length, a reflection, or a reliability rating.

items.prompt: Because the signal travels slower than light, a cable also has a small:
choices: Propagation delay (the signal takes real time to cross)
Increase in the speed of light
Negative length
Color shift
items.explanation: Finite travel speed means a real, if tiny, delay from one end to the other, the propagation delay. It does not change the speed of light or the cable's length.

items.prompt: If a load doesn't match the line, what is a common fix besides replacing it?
choices: Add a matching network to nudge the impedance back to a match
Make the cable a different color
Cool the cable down
Cut the cable in half
items.explanation: A small matching network adjusts the impedance so the system matches and power is delivered, without swapping the load itself. Color, temperature, and length are not the fix.

items.prompt: What is the Smith chart, mentioned as the next sub-topic?
choices: A circular graph that makes impedance matching visual
A type of coax connector
A brand of cable
A unit for measuring power
items.explanation: The Smith chart is a circular plotting tool used to design impedance matches visually. It is the focus of the next sub-topic; here we just point forward to it.
